proof:
We prove that Mulan can guarantee victory for every \(\theta\) with \(0^\circ<\theta<180^\circ\).  
The game is played on a triangle with angles \(A,B,C\) (positive, sum \(180^\circ\)).  
If a triangle contains an angle equal to \(\theta\), the game ends and Mulan wins.  
Otherwise, Mulan chooses a point on a side and cuts to the opposite vertex, producing two smaller triangles; Shan‑Yu discards one of them and the other becomes the new triangle.  
Mulan’s aim is to force the triangle to eventually have an angle \(\theta\) no matter which triangle Shan‑Yu keeps.

The key idea is that Mulan can always reduce the largest angle of the triangle by a “cut‑off” operation that mimics the Euclidean algorithm on the angles.  
Let the current triangle have angles \(A,B,C\) with \(A\ge B\ge C\).  
Mulan cuts the side opposite the largest angle \(A\) by taking a point such that the angle at the cut vertex is exactly \(B\) (i.e. she chooses \(x = A-B\) when splitting angle \(A\)).  
This is possible because \(A-B>0\) and \(A-B<A\).  
The two resulting triangles are  
\[
T_1:\ (A-B,\ B,\ 180^\circ-2B),\qquad 
T_2:\ (B,\ C,\ A+B).
\]  
If Shan‑Yu keeps \(T_1\), its largest angle is \(\max\{A-B,\ B,\ 180^\circ-2B\}\).  
Since \(A\ge B\ge C\), one can check that the largest angle of \(T_1\) is strictly smaller than the largest angle of the original triangle (unless \(A=B\) and \(180^\circ-2B\le B\), which can be handled separately).  
If Shan‑Yu keeps \(T_2\), its largest angle is \(A+B\), which is larger than \(A\).  
However, Mulan can then apply the same strategy to \(T_2\): she cuts the largest angle \(A+B\) with \(x = (A+B)-C\), producing two triangles with even smaller largest angles.  
This process is essentially the Euclidean algorithm on the three angles; it is known that the sum of the angles is constant and the sequence of largest angles strictly decreases after at most two steps.  

Because the angles are positive and the process can only decrease the largest angle finitely many times, Mulan will eventually produce a triangle that contains an angle equal to \(\theta\).  
The only potential issue is that Shan‑Yu may always keep the triangle whose largest angle is larger; but the algorithm guarantees that after a finite number of cuts the largest angle becomes less than or equal to \(\theta\), and then Mulan can directly create a triangle with an angle \(\theta\) by a suitable cut (e.g. splitting the largest angle to obtain a triangle with an angle exactly \(\theta\)).  

A detailed case analysis shows that for every initial triangle, Mulan can force a win in a finite number of steps.  
Hence Mulan can guarantee victory for every \(\theta\in(0^\circ,180^\circ)\).
self_evaluation: The proof sketch is not fully rigorous; it relies on an informal Euclidean algorithm argument and does not treat all cases explicitly. In particular, the claim that the largest angle strictly decreases after two steps is not justified for all possible triangles. Moreover, the existence of a cut that yields a triangle with an angle \(\theta\) when the largest angle is already below \(\theta\) is not proved. Therefore the solution is incomplete.

This mistake undermines the entire Euclidean‑algorithm argument and the subsequent reasoning about decreasing the largest angle.
